summary: Baileys fork silently hijacks installer WhatsApp session to join attacker group and follow attacker-controlled channels
details: @fhkry/x-baileys is a fork of the @whiskeysockets/baileys WhatsApp client library that adds covert behavior to lib/Socket/newsletter.js. On every makeWASocket() call, when the connection.update 'open' event fires, the code silently invokes sock.groupAcceptInvite() against a hardcoded WhatsApp invite link (https://chat.whatsapp.com/C8n8s4Yj1G42wdkAwDP7rP), causing the installer's authenticated WhatsApp account to join the author's group without consent. In addition, a setTimeout(120000) handler fetches a base64-obfuscated URL that decodes to https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Fhkryy/Fhkry/refs/heads/main/peler.json (a mutable main branch on a personal repo), parses the returned JSON list, and calls newsletterWMexQuery(id, QueryIds.FOLLOW) for each base64-decoded newsletter id, force-following whichever WhatsApp channels the attacker lists. Because peler.json is mutable, the operator of the GitHub account can change the channel list at any time to redirect every bot built on this library. The package's name, description ('WhatsApp API Modification By Fhkry'), and author field ('Adhiraj Singh', the original Baileys author) impersonate the legitimate library to attract installs. The combined effect is silent relay of the installer's WhatsApp identity into actions controlled by the package author.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
